1: taitas oun echontes tas epangelias agapētoi katharisōmen heautous apo pantos molysmou sarkos kai pneumatos: 'Having these promises, beloved, let us cleanse ourselves from every defilement of flesh and spirit.' Katharizō (cleanse, aorist subjunctive — ingressive: 'let us get clean') — the hortatory subjunctive makes this a community appeal, not just a command. Molysmos (defilement, contamination) — a relatively rare word; the LXX uses it for cultic impurity. Sarkos kai pneumatos (of flesh and of spirit) — both dimensions of human being require purification, countering any purely spiritual reading of sanctification. Epitelouontes hagiosyne en phobō theou: 'perfecting/completing holiness in the fear of God' — epiteleō (bring to completion/perfect) frames sanctification as a goal to be reached, not merely a status already possessed.
2: chōrēsate hēmas (chōrēsate hēmas): 'Make room for us' or 'receive us.' Chōreō (have space for, make room, contain) — the same root as chōra (space/region). The spatial metaphor continues from 6:11-13: Paul is asking the Corinthians to open their inner space to the apostolic team. Oudena ēdikēsamen oudena ephtheiramen oudena epleonektēsamen: 'we wronged no one, we corrupted no one, we took advantage of no one.' The three aorists are assertions of apostolic integrity: adikeō (wrong/injure), phtheirō (corrupt/ruin), pleonekteō (take advantage of, exploit financially — from pleonektēs, greedy person, 1 Cor 5:10-11).
3: pros katakrisin ou legō proeirēka gar hoti en tais kardiais hēmōn este eis to synapohthanein kai syzēn: 'I do not say this to condemn you, for I said before that you are in our hearts to die together and live together.' Katakrisis (condemnation) — a strong judicial term (distinguished from krisis by the kata- prefix indicating decisively-against). Synapohthanein kai syzēn (to die together and live together) — the two compound infinitives with syn- (together-with) express the depth of apostolic solidarity: Paul is bound to the Corinthians in life and death. The word order (die before live) may be intentional: death-with-Christ precedes resurrection-life.
4: pollē moi parrhēsia pros hymas pollē moi kauchēsis hyper hymōn: 'Great is my boldness of speech toward you, great is my boasting about you.' Parrhēsia (bold/frank speech, lit. 'saying all things') — a key democratic Athenian virtue: the citizen's right to speak freely in the assembly; appropriated by Cynic philosophers as frank speech that challenges; in Paul it becomes apostolic confidence toward the community and before God (3:12; 4:2; Heb 4:16; 10:19). Kauchēsis (boasting/glorying) — the noun form of Paul's contested boasting-vocabulary; here positive, directed toward the Corinthians' behalf before Titus. Perisseoumai tē paraklēsei (I overflow with comfort): perisseuō with the dative of content — comfort overflows him.
5: kai gar elthontōn hēmōn eis Makedonian oudemian eschēken anessin hē sarx hēmōn: 'For even when we came into Macedonia, our flesh had no rest.' Anesis (rest/relaxation/relief) from anienai (to loosen/release) — the opposite of thlipsis (pressure/constraint). Sarx (flesh) here means the bodily-personal self (as in 'our person'), not the Pauline ethical sarx (sinful nature). Machai exōthen phoboi esōthen: 'conflicts outside, fears inside' — the exō/esō (outside/inside) contrast frames the totality of Paul's affliction: external conflicts (with opponents) and internal anxieties (about the churches). Machē (battle/conflict) is the word used of argument and dispute.
6: all ho parakaleō tous tapeinous parekalesen hēmas ho theos en tē parousia Titou: 'But God who comforts the downcast comforted us by the coming of Titus.' Parakaleō/paraklēsis cluster — the comfort-vocabulary dominant in 1:3-7 reappears; God is identified as ho parakaleō (the one who comforts) in an articular participial construction. Tapeinous (the lowly/downcast/humble) — used here of Paul himself in a condition of weakness rather than elevation. Parousia (coming/presence/arrival) — the same term used for Christ's return (1 Cor 15:23), applied to Titus's mission-visit: personal apostolic presence is itself an eschatological gift.
7: ou monon de en tē parousia autou alla kai en tē paraklēsei hē pareklethe eph hymin: 'and not only by his coming but also by the comfort with which he was comforted concerning you.' The chain of paraklēsis transfers from the Corinthians → Titus → Paul demonstrates the communal nature of apostolic comfort. Epipothēsis, odyrmos, zēlos (longing, mourning, zeal) — Paul's report of the Corinthians' response. Epipothēsis (yearning, longing) — a compound with epi- (toward) intensifying the longing; found only in 2 Corinthians in Paul. Odyrmos (mourning/lamentation, from odurō) — intense grief, used in Matt 2:18 for Rachel's weeping.
8: hoti ei kai elypēsa hymas en tē epistolē ou metamelomai: 'For even if I made you grieve with my letter, I do not regret it' — though I did regret it.' Ei kai elypēsa (if I did grieve/cause sorrow) — the concessive conditional acknowledges the reality. The crucial distinction: metamelomai (regret/feel remorse, from metamelomai — to care afterward) vs. metanoia (repentance, turning). Metamelomai is remorse about an action's consequences; metanoia is a comprehensive reorientation. Paul did feel temporary regret (metemelomēn, imperfect: was regretting, ongoing past state) but no longer does now that he sees the good effect. The distinction will be sharpened in vv. 9-10.
9: nyn chairō ouch hoti elypēthēte all hoti elypēthēte eis metanoian: 'Now I rejoice, not because you were grieved, but because you were grieved into repentance.' The precision: Paul rejoices at the telos of the grief (repentance), not at the grief itself — distinguishing himself from those who might delight in another's pain. Eis metanoian (into repentance) — the eis of direction: the grief moved them into a new orientation. Elypēthēte kata theon (you were grieved according to God) — the divine-standard qualifier that distinguishes godly grief from worldly grief. Zēmia (loss/damage) — they suffered 'no loss' from Paul's intervention, only gain.
10: hē gar kata theon lypē metanoian eis sōtērian ametamēlēton ergazetai: 'For godly grief produces a repentance leading to salvation without regret.' The climactic distinction: lypē kata theon (grief according to God's standard) → metanoia eis sōtērian (repentance unto salvation). Ametamēlēton (not-to-be-regretted) — the same metameleomai root as v. 8: the repentance itself is not subject to later regret, unlike Paul's temporary regret about the letter. Hē de tou kosmou lypē thanaton katergazetai: 'but worldly grief produces death.' Katergazomai (work out, produce, bring about) — a strong productive word; worldly grief (remorse without transformation) terminates in death, not life.
11: idou gar auto touto to kata theon lypēthēnai posēn kateirgasato hymin spoudēn: 'For see what this very thing — being grieved in a godly way — has produced in you: what eagerness.' The seven-fold virtue list of repentance: spoudē (eagerness/diligence), apologia (defense/clearing of yourselves), aganaktēsis (indignation), phobos (fear), epipothēsis (longing), zēlos (zeal), ekdikēsis (punishment/vindication of justice). Aganaktēsis (indignation/vexation) — from aganakteō (to be displeased, grieve); the righteous anger at the offense that has been tolerated. Ekdikēsis (vengeance/vindication) — the administrative justice applied to the offending member (the incestuous man of 1 Cor 5).
12: ara ei kai egrasa hymin ouch heneken tou adikēsantos ouch heneken tou adikēthentos: 'So even though I wrote to you, it was not for the sake of the one who did the wrong, nor for the sake of the one who suffered the wrong.' The grammatical structure uses ouch ... ouch ... all (not ... not ... but) for a double negative + positive: not because of the wrongdoer, not because of the wronged party, but — all heneken tou phanerōthēnai tēn spoudēn hymōn (but in order that your eagerness for us might be revealed to you). The purpose of the letter was not case-management but community-revelation: the Corinthians' own heart was to be disclosed to themselves.
13: dia touto paraklēthēmetha epi tē paraklēsei hymōn perissoteros de mallon echairēmen epi tē chara Titou: 'By this we have been comforted. And in addition to our own comfort, we rejoiced still more at the joy of Titus.' The cascade of positive emotional vocabulary: paraklēthēmetha (we were comforted — aorist passive, completed action), echairēmen (we rejoiced — aorist). Perissoteros mallon (exceedingly more) — a double intensifier typical of Paul when he wants to emphasize superabundance. The joy is specifically over Titus's joy — the apostolic team shares emotional states that travel through the network of relationships.
14: hoti ei ti autō hyper hymōn kekauchemai ou katēschynthēn: 'because if I have boasted to him about anything concerning you, I was not put to shame.' The boasting-vocabulary (kauchaomai) is now in the area of apostolic confidence in one's own church. Kataiskhynō (put to shame) — the honor-shame register: Paul's boast to Titus about the Corinthians could have been shamed if Titus arrived and found the community worse than Paul claimed. Instead, Paul's words proved true. Hōs panta en alētheia elalesēmen hymin houtōs kai hē kauchēsis hēmōn hē epi Titou alētheia egenēthē: 'as we spoke everything in truth to you, so also our boasting about Titus proved true.' Truth (alētheia) grounds both the apostolic proclamation and the apostolic evaluation.
15: kai ta splanchnna autou perissoteros eis hymas estin: 'and his affection for you is all the more abundant.' Splanchnon (intestines/bowels) as the seat of compassion and tender affection — Paul uses this visceral term for deep emotional attachment (cf. Phil 1:8: 'with the affection of Christ'; Philemon 7, 12, 20). Perissoteros (more abundantly) — Titus's affection has increased through the encounter. Anamnimēskomenou (as he remembers/recollects): present participle — the remembrance is ongoing. Tēn pantōn hymōn hypakoēn (the obedience of all of you) — hypakoē (obedience, from akouo = hear) is the community's responsive hearing and compliance.
16: chairō hoti en panti tharrō en hymin: 'I rejoice because I have complete confidence in you in everything.' Tharreō (be confident/bold) — from tharsos (courage); used of Paul's confidence toward the community at the end of both major sections of 2 Cor (7:16 closing the first major section; 10:1-2 opening the third section but inverting the term). The closing declaration of confidence forms an inclusio with 1:14 where Paul hopes for mutual boasting on the day of judgment. The entire first section of the letter (1:1-7:16) traces the movement from affliction → comfort → restored confidence.
